user_input: PREVIOUS WORKS Among the relevant published studies in the log-based anomaly detection space, sequence-based is one of the most commonly used approaches. Therefore, there are limited relevant references as this study employs a point-based approach. Below are the relevant baselines from previous research: 1) Pylogsentiment is the first study which employs sentiment analysis-based anomaly detection on operat- ing system logs using GRU and GloVe embedding. 2) SentiLog , similar to , use a two-layered BiLSTM and GloVe embedding model to perform anomaly detection on parallel file system logs. 3) TransSentLog is a further development of and uses a two-layered transformer encoder which used two attention heads and GloVe embedding along with integrated gradients to add explainability to the trained model.

Therefore, this paper proposes a multitask-based multiclass drone anomaly detection to assist in a forensic investigation. The main contributions of this paper are summarized as follows: 1) We propose DroneLog, an interpretable sentiment- based multiclass drone flight log anomaly detection framework that includes three anomaly severity levels: low, medium, and high. Providing the severity level can provide better detection results for the investigator to pinpoint the event of interest. In this section, challenges encountered during the study are disclosed, along with limitations and threats to validity. As drone forensics is an emerging topic, there are very limited public datasets available. The log messages used in this study are mainly acquired from DJI-made devices. There is yet to come a publicly available dataset of log messages from other drone manufacturers. Several publicly available datasets are mainly about sensor data and multimedia artefacts . This research advances IT governance practices by providing organizations with a valuable tool to automate and improve IT strategies, effectively achieving business objectives. user_input: I. INTRODUCTION I. INTRODUCTION The use of drones has become increasingly common in various applications, including aerial photography, surveying, and monitoring. However, drones can also be involved in incidents such as accidents, violations, or even crimes, making it necessary to investigate drone logs as part of forensic investigations. In a forensic investigation, drone logs are essential sources of information that can help investigators reconstruct the events leading up to an incident. Drone logs typically include information such as flight paths, altitude, speed, battery life, sensor data, and other telemetry information . Important analysis of drone logs involves the identification of the most critical pieces of information in the log data and the analysis of how they relate to the incident

reference: I. INTRODUCTION I. INTRODUCTION The use of drones has become increasingly common in various applications, including aerial photography, surveying, and monitoring. However, drones can also be involved in incidents such as accidents, violations, or even crimes, making it necessary to investigate drone logs as part of forensic investigations. In a forensic investigation, drone logs are essential sources of information that can help investigators reconstruct the events leading up to an incident. Drone logs typically include information such as flight paths, altitude, speed, battery life, sensor data, and other telemetry information . CONCLUSION AND FUTURE WORK In this paper, we demonstrate how to train a log-based anomaly detection model that can prioritise the prediction of higher severity anomalies on drone flight logs while increasing the prediction confidence score at the same time. Considering the nature of the dataset, where samples that belong to different severity levels share common features, a multitask vector label representation along with severity-oriented decoding is proposed. In the future, we plan to explore the flight logs of other drone models to train more general anomaly detection models. CONCLUSION This research demonstrates that the YOLOv8 model, specifically the YOLOv8m variant, provides excellent results in detecting Peritoneal Carcinomatosis (PC) from the used laparoscopic image dataset. By utilizing and fine-tuning the pre-trained YOLOv8 weights, this model shows superior performance with a mean Average Precision (mAP50) of 0.871, precision of 0.893, recall of 0.785, and an F1 score of 0.84. The predicted images also show that the performance of YOLOv8 with pre-trained weights is able to predict images well. All YOLOv8 scales are accurate in making predictions, with no errors in image prediction. In contrast, without using pre-trained weights, YOLOv8n and YOLOv8s still make errors in prediction, with some lesions being missed.

response: This systematic literature review highlights the application of deep learning in lesion detection using endoscopic and laparoscopic modalities, which is relevant to the findings of the YOLOv8 model's performance in detecting Peritoneal Carcinomatosis.
reference: papers using private, public, and both datasets. 2) PRETRAINED WEIGHTS Pretrained weights , generated from previous training on large datasets like COCO or ImageNet , are particu- larly useful when training data is limited. Models pretrained on these datasets have optimized weights for recognizing common features, mitigating issues associated with small datasets. Transfer learning and fine-tuning allow models to adapt to new datasets, significantly reducing training time. Studies utilizing pretrained models include for detect- ing polyps with YOLOv4, for Crohn’s disease lesions, for pre-colorectal cancer abnormalities, for bladder cancer lesions, and for cervical lesions. The study also fine-tuned pretrained weights on the Kaggle dataset. Moreover, pretrained weights from the COCO dataset were used in studies like for gastric cancer lesions
